If the distress is strong or persistent, seeing a healthcare professional is important and entirely legitimate.

steps: Watch for persistent fatigue, loss of motivation, and irritability.
Don't minimise the signals — take them seriously early.
Lighten your load and bring back sleep, sport, and rest.
Temporarily lower your objectives while you recover.
Talk to someone and reach out to student health services.

faq.q: How do I know if it's normal tiredness or real burnout?
faq.a: If rest is no longer enough to recharge you and it's been going on for weeks, it's no longer passing fatigue. See someone without waiting.

faq.q: Will asking for help hurt my studies?
faq.a: On the contrary. Acting early protects your health and your ability to finish. Untreated burnout is what truly derails a course of study.

title: Nail a Group Oral Presentation
intro: A group presentation isn't just a series of solo talks bolted together. Cohesion and transitions are what separate a good one from a great one.

sections.heading: Build a shared thread
sections.body: The classic flaw in group presentations is the patchwork effect: each person recites their part with no connection to the others. To avoid it, build the overall plan and a clear narrative thread together. Every member should know the whole presentation, not just their section. The audience should experience one unified presentation, not three separate talks stitched together. Coherence is prepared collectively, in advance.

sections.heading: Nail the transitions
sections.body: The handoff between speakers is a fragile moment. Prepare proper transitions: the person finishing briefly signals what's coming next; the person taking over picks it up. Avoid awkward silences and 'uh, I think it's your turn now'. Well-rehearsed transitions create an impression of control and show that the group genuinely worked together.

sections.heading: Rehearse together
sections.body: Each practising alone isn't enough. Do at least one complete run-through as a group, in order, timed. You'll catch repetitions, time imbalances, and shaky transitions. This collective rehearsal also shows who is confident and who needs support. It's the step that separates an average presentation from a smooth, convincing one.

steps: Build a shared plan and narrative thread together.
Make sure everyone knows the full presentation.
Prepare clear transitions between each speaker.
Do at least one full timed run-through together.
Align your visual aids for a coherent overall presentation.

faq.q: How do we handle someone who talks too much or too little?
faq.a: Sort it out in rehearsal — adjust the length of each section to balance everyone's speaking time calmly, ahead of time.

title: Conduct Reliable Research for an Assignment
intro: For a report or dissertation, knowing how to find and filter information is as valuable as knowing how to write it up. Not all sources are equal.

sections.heading: Start from a clear question
sections.body: Before searching, formulate precisely what you want to know. A vague question leads to hours of scattered reading. Define your topic, its limits, its sub-questions. This initial clarity guides your research, helps you recognise relevant sources, and filter out what strays from the point. Good searching always starts with knowing what you're actually looking for.

sections.heading: Evaluate the reliability of sources
sections.body: Not all web pages are equal. For every source, ask who wrote it, when, for what purpose, and whether it cites its own references. Favour books, academic articles, institutional websites, and specialist publications. Be wary of anonymous, dated, or clearly biased content. Cross-referencing several credible sources on the same point protects you from errors and one-sided information.

sections.heading: Cite your sources
sections.body: Using an idea or data without indicating the origin is plagiarism, which is heavily penalised. Systematically note where each piece of information comes from as you read — you'll never want to track it down again later. Learn the citation format expected in your discipline. Citing sources doesn't weaken your work; it shows your rigour and anchors your argument on solid ground.

steps: Formulate a specific, clearly bounded research question.
Prioritise books, academic articles, and institutional sources.
Check the author, date, and intent behind each source.
Cross-reference several reliable sources on key points.
Note references as you go to cite correctly.

faq.q: Can I use Wikipedia?
faq.a: As a starting point to get oriented and spot other sources, yes. But cite the credible references it links to at the bottom of the page, not Wikipedia itself.

title: Use AI to Learn, Not to Cheat
intro: AI tools can accelerate your learning or sabotage it, depending on how you use them. The line comes down to what your brain is actually doing.

sections.heading: An assistant, not a substitute
sections.body: Having an AI write a full essay and submitting it as your own is fraud — and more importantly, you learn nothing. On the other hand, AI is invaluable as an assistant: getting a confusing explanation rephrased, generating quiz questions to practise with, checking your understanding. The simple rule: the tool should help you think, never think for you.

sections.heading: Uses that actually build skills
sections.body: Ask the AI to explain a concept from a different angle, give you examples, fire quiz questions at you, or list the key points to revise. You can have it play examiner and critique your essay outline. In all these cases, you're doing the intellectual work — the AI is just creating opportunities for you to practise.

sections.heading: Stay critical
sections.body: AI can be wrong, can fabricate facts, and can confidently cite sources that don't exist. Never take its answers at face value — verify important information against your course or reliable sources. Also be aware that your institution has rules on AI use. Find out what they are and follow them, both to avoid trouble and to actually learn something.

steps: Use AI to practise and learn — never to produce work for you.
Ask it for alternative explanations and quiz questions.
Always verify important facts against reliable sources.
Find out your institution's rules on AI use.
Make sure your brain is doing the actual work.

faq.q: Is it cheating to use AI for revision?
faq.a: No, if it helps you understand and practise. It becomes cheating when you have it produce a graded piece of work you present as your own.

title: Succeed in Your First Days of an Internship
intro: The start of an internship sets the tone. A curious, reliable attitude from day one leaves a lasting impression on everyone supervising you.

sections.heading: Observe and integrate
sections.body: In the first few days, you don't know the codes, the tools, or the people — and that's expected. Observe how the team works: schedules, communication style, level of formality. Introduce yourself to the people you meet, remember names, be on time. A successful integration starts with this phase of attentive listening before rushing in to change everything.

sections.heading: Ask questions intelligently
sections.body: Nobody expects you to know everything — you're there to learn. Questions are valued. But take notes on the answers so you don't ask the same thing ten times. Before asking someone, check whether you can find the answer yourself. Thoughtful, well-spaced questions show you're a fast learner who respects colleagues' time.

sections.heading: Be reliable
sections.body: Beyond skills, what stands out in an internship is reliability: being on time, following through on what you said you'd do, flagging difficulties in advance, delivering careful work. Even on modest tasks, the care you bring is noticed. A reliable, committed intern leaves an excellent impression — useful when you need a reference or want to be hired.

steps: Observe the team's norms, tools, and communication style.
Introduce yourself, remember names, be punctual.
Ask thoughtful questions and take notes on the answers.
Take care over even the most modest tasks.
Keep your commitments and flag problems before they escalate.

faq.q: What should I do if I'm not given any work?
faq.a: Go to your supervisor, show you're available, and offer to help. Better to ask for tasks than to wait passively at your desk.

title: Ask for a Letter of Recommendation
intro: A genuine recommendation from a teacher or internship supervisor carries real weight in an application. The key is asking for it the right way.

sections.heading: Choose the right person
sections.body: The best recommendation comes from someone who genuinely knows you and can speak concretely about your work: a teacher whose class you took seriously, a satisfied internship supervisor. A prestigious name who barely knows you will produce a vague, lukewarm letter. Go for the person who can give specific examples of your qualities.

sections.heading: Ask with tact and plenty of notice
sections.body: Ask in person or with a carefully drafted message — never at the last minute. Writing a strong letter takes time: give several weeks' notice. Clearly explain what it's for, and don't hesitate to provide your CV, the opportunity you're targeting, and a reminder of what you did together. You're making the task as easy as possible for them.

sections.heading: Stay courteous afterwards
sections.body: A recommendation is a favour, not an entitlement. Whether the person agrees or not, thank them. If they agree, let them know how the application goes — that shows respect and keeps the relationship alive. Someone who's been properly thanked and kept in the loop will happily recommend you again. The courtesy around the request matters as much as the request itself.

steps: Pick someone who genuinely knows your work.
Ask well in advance — several weeks before the deadline.
Explain what the letter is for and what you're applying for.
Provide your CV, the opportunity, and a reminder of your work together.
Thank the person and let them know the outcome.

faq.q: What if a teacher says no?
faq.a: Respect the answer without pushing — they may be short on time or feel they don't know you well enough. Thank them and ask someone else.

title: Understand the Job Market in Your Sector
intro: Choosing a path without knowing its real prospects means moving blind. A little research illuminates the decisions that matter most.

sections.heading: Research actual job prospects
sections.body: Before committing to a programme, find out what it actually opens up: types of positions, sectors that are hiring, how competitive the market is, typical starting salary ranges. This information exists in graduate outcome surveys, official career fact sheets, and feedback from alumni. It shouldn't dictate your choice, but it should inform it and prevent wishful thinking.

sections.heading: Spot the skills employers want
sections.body: Reading job ads in your sector — even long before you're job-hunting — is incredibly instructive. You'll spot the skills actually in demand, the tools to master, the profiles that stand out. You can then steer your elective choices, internships, and projects to develop those strengths during your studies, rather than discovering what employers want only after graduating.

sections.heading: Keep a nuanced perspective
sections.body: The job market evolves: new roles emerge, others transform. Don't base your choice solely on a current trend that may have shifted by the time you graduate. Equally, don't rely only on received wisdom about a given sector. Cross-reference multiple sources, talk to practitioners currently working in the field, and remember that transferable skills stay valuable everywhere.

steps: Check graduate outcome data and career fact sheets for your programme.
Read job ads in your sector regularly.
Spot the most in-demand skills and tools.
Steer your electives, internships, and projects towards those skills.
Cross-reference sources and talk to people currently in the field.

faq.q: Should I pick a career just because it has lots of openings?
faq.a: Prospects matter, but some baseline of interest is essential to last. Look for the intersection between what you like and what the market needs.

title: Talk About Money With Employers Without Freezing Up
intro: Discussing pay with an employer often intimidates young people. With the right prep, the conversation is normal — and goes better than you'd fear.

sections.heading: Do your homework before the conversation
sections.body: Before any discussion about pay, find out what's standard: the legal minimum internship stipend, typical entry-level salaries for your role and region. These benchmarks give you a realistic range to work with. Going in with figures in mind — neither naively low nor wildly high — means you can discuss calmly rather than being caught off guard or asking for the impossible.

sections.heading: Pick the right moment and tone
sections.body: The pay question is best raised late in the process, once the employer is genuinely interested. Ask it calmly, as a legitimate piece of information, without aggression or apology. You can ask what range they have in mind before stating your own expectation. A composed, professional tone shows you're taking it seriously without turning it into a standoff.

sections.heading: Consider the full package
sections.body: Pay is only part of what a position offers. For a first job or internship, the experience gained, the quality of the supervision, the career prospects, and the substance of the work all count enormously. A slightly lower-paid role that teaches you a great deal can be a better long-term investment. Evaluate the whole offer, not just the number.

steps: Research the standard pay for your role and region.
Prepare a realistic range — not naively low, not wildly high.
Raise the topic calmly, late in the process.
Ask for their range before stating your expectation.
Assess the full package: responsibilities, supervision, prospects.

faq.q: Can you negotiate a first salary?
faq.a: Often there's some room, especially for a permanent contract. Stay measured and reasoned. For internships, the stipend is more regulated with less room to move.
